description: Figure 3 presents TEM characterization of the Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high-entropy steel powder after 35 h of mechanical alloying. Panel (a) shows a bright-field TEM image with a 20 nm scale bar, revealing a nanocrystalline microstructure composed of ultrafine grains formed during prolonged milling. Panel (b) displays the corresponding selected area electron diffraction pattern with a 10 1/nm scale bar, which exhibits distinct concentric polycrystalline rings. The caption and annotation identify these rings as originating from the BCC phase, with specific reflections from the (110), (200), and (211) planes, as marked by dashed blue lines. The polycrystalline ring pattern confirms the fine-grained nature of the alloy and establishes that the BCC crystal structure is the dominant phase in the 35 h milled powder. Collectively, these micrographs demonstrate that mechanical alloying for 35 h successfully produces a nanocrystalline BCC microstructure, corroborating the XRD findings of nanoscale grain formation and providing direct morphological and crystallographic evidence for the phase evolution during milling.
